# Transcrição: 07-Linking

- **Fonte:** 07-Linking
- **Duração:** 15:40
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

I hope you've taken the time to take your stories and then put them into the first two steps of the formula, the incident and the point. Now we're getting to the third step. The third step in the storytelling process is probably one of the reasons why most people don't story tell. And they don't story tell because they don't know what it is and they don't know how to tell stories effectively. Because to make a story effective and not just entertaining or engaging, to make it effective you need the third step. And the third step is linking. And this is where you make the story relevant to the person that you're talking to. Often people don't story tell because they don't know how to make the story relevant and they think in their own heads, I shouldn't story tell because it's not relevant. It's not relevant in my line of work. It's not relevant in my profession. So that kind of excuse that we all use as to why we don't story tell is simply because we don't know what the third step is. And storytelling only becomes powerful when you've got all three steps. You've got the incident that's set up well, who, what, where, when. Vax, bring the characters to life, keep it within two to three minutes. And then you've got a good point to the story. You have to then be able to link the story and make it relevant to the person you're sharing the story with. And the statement I'm going to make here is that when you learn how to link, what this means is that you can take any story you have in your life and almost use it in any situation. Now, I say almost because it's like those bacteria killing sprays you can get, 99.9999999999% of germs get killed when you use this spray. I'm going to use that same stat because there are times on the off chance you can't link a certain story to a certain situation. However, when you learn linking, you'll be able to link pretty much any story to pretty much any situation. Now before you learn how to link any story to any situation, you have to learn first of all how to link one word to another first. So we'll start with something simple. We'll say the word, say we say life is like. And let's say we named a random fruit. Let's say we just said life is like, we won't say lemons because that's too easy. We'll say life is like a pineapple. Life is like a pineapple. And when you look at this, that doesn't really make sense, does it? No, it doesn't make sense. I mean, what do you mean life is like a pineapple? And the reason this doesn't make sense is because you're trying to make a direct connection between the word life and pineapple. And when you're trying to make a direct connection, it doesn't work. So what we do is we make an indirect connection. And what you have to do there first is you have to get rid of the word life. And now just think of what words symbolize a pineapple? What words symbolize a pineapple? And start to brainstorm a list of words that could symbolize pineapple.

The reason I'm telling you this is because it has all the ingredients. Check this out. But before you do, I got to do that. I want to tell you a story. ## [15:08 - 15:40]

It doesn't have to be a secret and you don't have to be a keynote speaker to use this. This is a superpower if you learn how to do this masterfully. And here's the great thing. If you make the decision to learn this skill and build on this skill set now, it's going to serve you for the remainder of your life regardless of what career you move into. Take this skill set as a skill set that helps separate you from the rest as you move forward in the future. Learn it once now, master it and it serves you for the remainder of your life.
